socratic.0:
Socrates. You are about to undertake the practical investigation for Unit 4. Tell me plainly: whose work is it?
Student. Mine. I design it, I conduct it, and I analyse it myself.
Socrates. And from where do you draw your question?
Student. From one of three areas — fields, motion or light — and it must involve a single continuous independent variable, so I generate my own primary data.
Socrates. Then how is the labour recorded and finally shown?
Student. Everything goes into a logbook as I work, and I report it as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words.
Socrates. Good. Then let us walk through it in order — what an investigation is, how to measure well, how data become evidence, and how the whole thing is concluded and communicated. Shall we begin?
Student. Lead on.
socratic.1:
Connects Socrates. If you choose a fields question, where will the underlying physics come from?
Student. From Action without contact and Electricity from fields; a light question rests on Light, matter, relativity, and a motion question on the Unit 3 study of motion.
Socrates. So is the physics itself new to you?
Student. No — the physics is old ground. What is new is the discipline of generating evidence and then defending it.
socratic.2:
Socrates. Where, then, does an investigation begin?
Student. With an aim, or a question.
Socrates. Could the question be "is light beautiful?"
Student. No — it must be answerable by measurement, and it must isolate one relationship: how a single continuous quantity depends on another.
Socrates. Name me the roles. The quantity you deliberately change — what is it?
Student. The independent variable. The quantity I measure in response is the dependent variable.
Socrates. And everything else that might meddle?
Student. Each such quantity is a controlled variable, held constant throughout. And here the independent variable must be continuous — able to take any value across a range, not just fixed categories.
socratic.3:
Socrates. Before you collect a single reading, you commit to a prediction. What do we call it, and what must it rest on?
Student. A hypothesis — a testable prediction of the relationship between the independent and dependent variables, stated beforehand and grounded in the relevant physics.
Socrates. Now tell me honestly: do you run the experiment to confirm it?
Student. No — I run it to test it. The data may support it, partially support it, or fail to support it, and I must be willing to accept any of those.
socratic.4:
Socrates. Let us separate two words that students often blur. What is the methodology?
Student. The overall type of investigation — here, a controlled practical experiment that generates primary data.
Socrates. And the method?
Student. The specific procedure: the equipment, the measurements, the range of the independent variable, how many repeated readings I take, and how I analyse it all.
Socrates. You said primary data. Whose data are those?
Student. Mine — data I generate. They can be quantitative, as measured numerical values, or qualitative, as recorded observations.
socratic.5:
Key idea Socrates. When is an experiment valid?
Student. When it investigates what it claims to investigate.
Socrates. And what could rob it of that?
Student. If a controlled variable is let loose, the dependent variable answers to more than one cause; or if my own bias as observer creeps into the readings. Either way the validity is broken.
socratic.6:
Socrates. Now to measuring. Can any measurement be exact?
Student. No measurement is exact.
Socrates. Then what would the true value be?
Student. The value I would find if the quantity could be measured perfectly.
Socrates. And a real reading stands apart from it. What do we name that gap?
Student. The difference between a measurement result and the true value is the measurement error.
socratic.7:
Socrates. Are accuracy and precision the same thing?
Student. No — and treating them as synonyms is a common slip.
Socrates. Define accuracy for me.
Student. It is qualitative: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value. I write "more accurate" or "less accurate" — never an accuracy as a number.
Socrates. And precision?
Student. How close my repeated measurements are to one another — the spread of the readings. It says nothing about closeness to the true value.
Socrates. So can readings be precise yet wrong?
Student. Yes — precise yet inaccurate, or accurate on average yet imprecise. The two travel independently.
socratic.8:
Socrates. An instrument has two further properties, also distinct. What is its resolution?
Student. The smallest change in the measured quantity that produces a perceptible change in the reading — a millimetre ruler resolves to 1 mm, a stopwatch reading hundredths resolves to 0.01 s.
Socrates. And its uncertainty — what does that tell you?
Student. The interval in which the true value is expected to lie. So 20 ± 1 mm means the true value is expected between 19 and 21 mm.
Socrates. By VCE convention, how large is an instrument's uncertainty?
Student. Half its smallest scale increment — so a millimetre ruler reads to ±0.5 mm and a 0.01 s stopwatch to ±0.005 s.
Socrates. Then may resolution and uncertainty be reported as if they were one quantity?
Student. Never. They are different things and must be kept apart.
socratic.9:
Trap Socrates. A student writes that a millimetre ruler's uncertainty is 1 mm. Where has she erred?
Student. She has used the increment itself. The uncertainty is half the smallest scale increment — the ruler resolves to 1 mm but its uncertainty is only ±0.5 mm.
socratic.10:
Socrates. Errors come in kinds, sorted by how they act on the readings. What is a random error?
Student. An unpredictable variation that scatters readings about the mean. It harms precision, and I tame it by repeating the measurement and averaging.
Socrates. And a systematic error?
Student. It pushes every reading off the true value in the same direction by about the same amount — from calibration, the instrument, or the method, like parallax or a zero error. It harms accuracy.
Socrates. Now the crucial question: will averaging cure both?
Student. Only the random one. Averaging cannot touch a systematic error, because every reading is shifted the same way by the same fault — the average is shifted with them.
socratic.11:
Trap Socrates. If repeating and averaging improves precision, has it fixed everything?
Student. No. It reduces random error, but a consistent offset survives the average untouched.
Socrates. So what must be done with that offset instead?
Student. The fault itself has to be found and removed — averaging will never do it for me.
socratic.12:
Socrates. Repeating measurements lets you make two judgements. What is repeatability?
Student. The closeness of agreement between successive measurements under the same conditions — same observer, instrument, method and place, over a short time. It evaluates precision.
Socrates. And if you change those conditions?
Student. Then I'm testing reproducibility — agreement when a different observer, instrument, method or location is used.
Socrates. Why is that second test so valuable?
Student. Because it checks a claimed precision and can expose a systematic error, tying the result back to accuracy.
socratic.13:
Socrates. And before any of this — before the first reading — what claims your attention?
Student. The health, safety and ethical guidelines: I identify the hazards, control the risks, and conduct the whole investigation responsibly.
socratic.14:
Socrates. We turn now to evidence. What is it?
Student. Data that bear on a hypothesis, a model or a theory, and that may support or refute it.
Socrates. From where do your primary data get their authority?
Student. From how they are recorded — the logbook.
Socrates. Describe it.
Student. A dated, contemporaneous record of every reading, observation, instrument setting and change of plan, written as I go. It proves the data are my own and lets the work be checked.
socratic.15:
Socrates. When you report numbers, the significant-figure conventions bind you. In a sum or difference, what governs the result?
Student. It keeps the fewest decimal places of any value I used.
Socrates. And in a product or quotient?
Student. It keeps the fewest significant figures.
Socrates. When do you round?
Student. Only at the final step — never on intermediate values, so rounding error cannot accumulate.
Socrates. And the symbols and units?
Student. Correct symbols, units and standard abbreviations, in accepted notation — such as m s⁻¹.
socratic.16:
Socrates. How do you make a pattern visible?
Student. By graphing — independent variable on the horizontal axis, dependent variable on the vertical.
Socrates. What does each point carry?
Student. Uncertainty bars: the vertical bar for uncertainty in the dependent variable, the horizontal for the independent, with the true value expected to lie within them.
Socrates. Now the line of best fit — through the first and last points?
Student. No. It follows the trend of all the points, passing within the uncertainty bars. It isn't forced through any chosen point or the origin, and it isn't extended beyond the data.
Socrates. Suppose a line you expect through the origin misses it. What does that reveal?
Student. A systematic error.
socratic.17:
Key idea Socrates. If your relationship curves, must you abandon the straight line?
Student. No — I linearise it. I transform a variable so the curve becomes a straight line, and then the gradient equals a physical quantity.
Socrates. What must travel with the transformed variable?
Student. Its axis units. If I plot t2 against distance, the axis reads "time2 (s2)".
Socrates. And the gradient — how do you take it honestly?
Student. From two points lying on the line, chosen far apart; its units are the vertical-axis units divided by the horizontal-axis units, and I include any ×10n axis scale factor.
socratic.18:
Socrates. A pattern now stands before you. Do you simply accept it?
Student. No — I evaluate it. I identify what caused the uncertainty in my readings, state the assumptions I made and the limitations of the data and method.
Socrates. To what end?
Student. To ask whether those things could account for any departure of my data from the expected relationship.
socratic.19:
Socrates. The conclusion arrives. What must it do?
Student. Answer the question, and state whether the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis.
Socrates. May you write that you have proved your hypothesis?
Student. No — "proves" and "disproves" are forbidden. A single investigation establishes evidence, not proof.
Socrates. And what else belongs there?
Student. The limitations of the investigation, its implications, perhaps a pointer to further work — but no new information that wasn't presented earlier.
socratic.20:
Socrates. Before you settle that conclusion, you owe the work a frank reckoning. What do you weigh?
Student. The limitations of the data and method — sources of random and systematic error, restrictions on the range or number of readings, and assumptions that may not hold.
Socrates. And having named each one?
Student. I propose a realistic improvement for it — not a wish, but something that could actually be done.
socratic.21:
Key idea Socrates. In what form is all this finally judged?
Student. As a scientific poster of no more than 600 words, with prescribed sections.
Socrates. Recite them in order.
Student. The title, which is the investigation question; my name; an introduction giving the aim, the hypothesis or prediction and the background physics; the methodology and methods; the results, shown to reveal the trend; a discussion that interprets and evaluates the data, states limitations and improvements, relates the result to the physics and links back to the hypothesis; a conclusion that answers the question; and the references and acknowledgements.
socratic.22:
Socrates. At the poster's heart sits one thing above all. What is it?
Student. A single Communication statement — one sentence answering the investigation question.
Socrates. How prominent?
Student. It takes up between 20 and 25 per cent of the poster's area, so a reader grasps the central finding at a glance.
Socrates. And the poster around it?
Student. It follows the conventions of science communication — correct terminology, symbols, equations, standard abbreviations, significant figures and units — and acknowledges its references succinctly.
socratic.23:
Connects Socrates. Give me a few questions you might actually pose.
Student. A fields one — how magnetic field strength varies with distance from a wire, drawing on Action without contact; a motion one — how a projectile's range depends on launch angle; a light one — how fringe spacing depends on slit separation, drawing on Light, matter, relativity.
Socrates. However different they look, what stays constant across them all?
Student. The discipline of this Area of Study — one continuous independent variable, controlled conditions, honest uncertainty. That never changes, whatever the question.
Socrates. Then you are ready to begin. Go and measure well.

On this page I set out, in order, what an investigation is, how to measure well, how data become evidence, and how the investigation is concluded and communicated.
curie.1: Connects You may conduct the investigation on any of the three content areas of the course. The physics is not new. What is new is the discipline of generating and defending evidence.
curie.2: Every investigation begins with an aim or a question. The question must be answerable by measurement and must isolate a single relationship: how one continuous quantity depends on another. The quantity you deliberately change is the independent variable; the quantity you measure in response is the dependent variable. Every other quantity that could affect the result is a controlled variable, and you hold it constant throughout. In this Area of Study the independent variable must be continuous, meaning it can take any value across a range rather than only fixed categories.
curie.3: A hypothesis is a testable prediction of the relationship between the independent and dependent variables, stated before you collect any data and grounded in relevant physics. You do not set out to confirm the hypothesis. You set out to test it, and the data may support, partially support or fail to support it.
curie.4: The methodology is the overall type of investigation you choose — here, a controlled practical experiment that generates primary data. curie.5: Key idea A valid experiment investigates what it claims to investigate. Validity is threatened when a controlled variable is allowed to change, so that the dependent variable responds to more than one cause, or when observer bias creeps into the readings.
curie.6: No measurement is exact. The true value of a quantity is the value you would find if you could measure it perfectly. A real reading differs from the true value, and the difference between a measurement result and the true value is the measurement error.
curie.7: Accuracy and precision are distinct, and I never treat them as synonyms. curie.9: Trap The uncertainty of an instrument is half its smallest scale increment, not the increment itself. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm but an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm.

You’re not out to confirm it. You’re out to test it — and the data are free to support it, partially support it, or knock it flat.
mack.4: The methodology is the overall kind of investigation you’ve chosen — here, a controlled practical experiment that generates primary data. The method is the nuts and bolts: the equipment, the measurements you take, the range of the independent variable, how many times you repeat each reading, and how you crunch the data afterwards. Primary data are data you generate yourself, and they come in two flavours: quantitative, as measured numerical values, or qualitative, as recorded observations.
mack.5: Key idea A valid experiment actually investigates what it claims to investigate — nothing more, nothing less. Validity takes a hit the moment a controlled variable is allowed to wander, so the dependent variable is now answering to more than one cause, or when observer bias creeps into the readings.
mack.6: No measurement is exact — not one. The true value of a quantity is the value you’d find if you could measure it perfectly. A real reading always misses, and the gap between a measurement result and the true value is the measurement error.
mack.7: Accuracy and precision are two different animals, and they are not synonyms. Accuracy is qualitative: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value, and you write “more accurate” or “less accurate” — never an accuracy as a number. Precision is how tightly your repeated measurements cluster around each other — the spread of the readings — and it tells you nothing about how close you are to the true value. A set of readings can be beautifully precise yet flat wrong, or accurate on average yet hopelessly scattered.
mack.8: An instrument has two telling traits — its resolution and its uncertainty — and these are different too. Resolution is the smallest change in the measured quantity that nudges the reading at all: a millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm and a stopwatch reading to hundredths a resolution of 0.01 s. The uncertainty states the interval the true value is expected to live in. A result written as 20 ± 1 mm means the true value is expected to lie somewhere in the interval 19–21 mm. By VCE convention the uncertainty of a measuring instrument is half its smallest scale increment, so a millimetre ruler reads to ±0.5 mm and a 0.01 s stopwatch to ±0.005 s. Resolution and uncertainty are not the same quantity, and you mustn’t report one as if it were the other.
mack.9: Trap The uncertainty of an instrument is half its smallest scale increment, not the increment itself. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm but an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm.
mack.10: Errors come in two kinds, sorted by how they shove the readings around. A random error is an unpredictable wobble that scatters readings about the mean; it hurts precision, and you beat it down by repeating the measurement and averaging. A systematic error tugs every reading off the true value in the same direction by roughly the same amount each time; it sneaks in through calibration, the instrument, or the method — think parallax or a zero error — and it hurts accuracy. Averaging only tames random error. It does nothing to a systematic error, because every single reading is shifted the same way by the same fault.
mack.11: Trap Repeating a measurement and averaging reduces random error and improves precision. It does not reduce systematic error: a consistent offset survives the average unchanged. The fault must instead be found and removed.
mack.12: Repeating measurements lets you make two related calls. Repeatability is how closely successive measurements agree under the same conditions — same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short stretch of time — and it sizes up precision. Reproducibility is how closely things agree once you change the conditions — a different observer, instrument, method or location — so it stress-tests a claimed precision and can flush out systematic error, tying the result back to accuracy.
mack.13: Before a single number gets collected, you apply the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines: spot the hazards, control the risks, and run the investigation like a responsible scientist.
mack.14: Evidence is data that bear on a hypothesis, a model or a theory — data that can support it or refute it. Primary data earn their authority from how they’re recorded. The logbook is a dated, in-the-moment record of every reading, observation, instrument setting and change of plan, written as the investigation unfolds. It vouches for the primary data as your own work and makes the whole thing checkable.
mack.15: When you report numbers, the significant-figures conventions kick in. In a sum or difference the result keeps the fewest decimal places of any value you used. In a product or quotient it keeps the fewest significant figures. Round only at the very last step, never on intermediate values, so rounding error doesn’t pile up on you. Report quantities with their correct symbols, units and standard abbreviations, written in the accepted notation — for instance, m s⁻¹.
mack.16: Graphing is where the patterns show themselves. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical. Each plotted point wears uncertainty bars: the vertical bar shows the uncertainty in the dependent variable and the horizontal bar the uncertainty in the independent variable, with the true value expected to lie inside the bars. A line of best fit is drawn to chase the trend of all the points, threading through the uncertainty bars — not strung between the first and last points only, not forced through any pet point or through the origin, and not stretched out beyond the range of your data. A line you’d expect to pass through the origin that misses it is waving a flag for systematic error.
mack.17: Key idea Linearisation straightens a curved relationship into a line, so that the gradient becomes a physical quantity you can read off. You transform the variable — and you transform its axis units right along with it — for example, plotting t2 against distance with the axis labelled “time2 (s2)”. Then read the gradient from two points sitting on the line and chosen far apart, take its units as the vertical-axis units divided by the horizontal-axis units, and don’t forget to fold in any ×10n axis scale factor.
mack.18: Once you’ve nailed down a pattern, you put it on trial. You name the causes of uncertainty in your readings, state the assumptions you leaned on and the limitations of both the data and the method, and ask whether any of these could account for the data drifting from the expected relationship.
mack.19: A conclusion answers the question and says straight out whether the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” stay out of it: a single investigation builds evidence, not proof. The conclusion also lays out the investigation’s limitations and its implications, and may point toward further work — but it smuggles in no new information that wasn’t already on the table.
mack.20: Before you lock in the conclusion, the data and method get audited for limitations — sources of random and systematic error, limits on the range or number of readings, and assumptions that might not hold — and you propose a realistic improvement for each one.
mack.21: Key idea The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. The required sections are: the title, which is your investigation question; your name; an introduction stating the aim, the hypothesis or prediction and the background physics; the methodology and methods; the results, laid out so the trend leaps out; a discussion that interprets and evaluates the data, states limitations and improvements, ties the result to the physics and links back to the hypothesis; a conclusion that answers the question; and the references and acknowledgements.
mack.22: Sitting dead centre on the poster is a single Communication statement — one sentence that answers the investigation question. It takes up between 20 and 25 per cent of the poster area, so a reader grabs the central finding in a single glance. The poster as a whole plays by the conventions of science communication — correct terminology, symbols, equations, standard abbreviations, significant figures and units — and acknowledges its references without fuss.
mack.23: Connects You can set the question in any content area you’ve studied. A fields investigation might measure how magnetic field strength falls off with distance from a wire, drawing on Action without contact; a motion investigation might measure how a projectile’s range depends on launch angle; a light investigation might measure how fringe spacing depends on slit separation, drawing on Light, matter, relativity. In every case the discipline of this Area of Study — one continuous independent variable, controlled conditions, honest uncertainty — stays exactly the same.

Here’s the catch: averaging only knocks down random error. It does nothing to systematic error, because every single reading has been shoved the same way by the same fault, so the shove survives the average.
feynman.11: Trap Repeating a measurement and averaging cuts down random error and sharpens your precision. But it does not touch systematic error: a consistent offset sails straight through the average untouched. To kill that, you have to hunt down the fault and remove it.
feynman.12: Repeating measurements lets you make two related calls. Repeatability is how closely successive measurements agree when nothing changes — same observer, instrument, method and location, all over a short time — and it’s a check on precision. Reproducibility is how closely things agree when you do change the conditions — a different observer, instrument, method or location — so it tests a claimed precision and can flush out systematic error, tying the result back to accuracy.
feynman.13: Before you collect a single number, you work through the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines: spotting the hazards, controlling the risks, and running the whole thing responsibly.
feynman.14: Evidence is data that bear on a hypothesis, a model or a theory — data that can support it or knock it down. Primary data get their authority from the way you record them. The logbook is a dated, on-the-spot record of every reading, observation, instrument setting and change of plan, written down as you go, while it’s happening. It vouches for the primary data as genuinely yours and lets anyone come along and check the work.
feynman.15: When you report numbers, the conventions of significant figures kick in. In a sum or a difference, the result keeps the fewest decimal places of any value you used. In a product or a quotient, it keeps the fewest significant figures. You round only at the very last step, never on the intermediate values, so that rounding error doesn’t pile up on you. And you report quantities with their correct symbols, units and standard abbreviations, in the accepted notation — for instance, m s⁻¹.
feynman.16: Patterns show up when you graph. You plot the independent variable on the horizontal axis and the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Every point carries its uncertainty bars: the vertical bar is the uncertainty in the dependent variable, the horizontal bar is the uncertainty in the independent variable, and the true value is expected to lie somewhere inside the bars. A line of best fit follows the trend of all the points, threading through the uncertainty bars; you do not just draw it through the first and last points, you do not force it through some favourite point or through the origin, and you do not stretch it past the range of your data. And if a line you’d expect to go through the origin doesn’t, that’s a systematic error talking.
feynman.17: Key idea Linearisation is the trick of turning a curved relationship into a straight line, so the gradient comes out as a physical quantity. You transform the variable, and you transform its axis units right along with it — for example, plotting t2 against distance with the axis labelled “time2 (s2)”. Then you read the gradient off two points that sit on the line and are far apart, its units are the vertical-axis units divided by the horizontal-axis units, and you fold in any ×10n axis scale factor.
feynman.18: Once you’ve got a pattern, you size it up. You pin down what’s causing the uncertainty in your readings, you state the assumptions you made and the limitations of both the data and the method, and you ask yourself whether any of that could explain why the data wandered off from the relationship you expected.
feynman.19: A conclusion answers the question and says whether the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. You don’t use the words “proves” and “disproves”: one investigation gives you evidence, not proof. The conclusion also spells out the limitations of the investigation and what it implies, and it may point toward further work — but it brings in no new information that wasn’t already on the table earlier.
feynman.20: Before you lock in the conclusion, you go back over the data and method looking for limitations — sources of random and systematic error, limits on the range or number of readings, and assumptions that might not hold up — and for each one you propose a realistic improvement.
feynman.21: Key idea The whole investigation gets assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. The required sections are: the title, which is your investigation question; your name; an introduction giving the aim, the hypothesis or prediction and the background physics; the methodology and methods; the results, laid out so the trend jumps out; a discussion that interprets and evaluates the data, gives limitations and improvements, ties the result to the physics and links back to the hypothesis; a conclusion that answers the question; and the references and acknowledgements.
feynman.22: Right in the middle of the poster sits a single Communication statement — one sentence that answers the investigation question. It takes up between 20 and 25 per cent of the poster area, so a reader gets the central finding in one glance. The poster as a whole sticks to the conventions of science communication — correct terminology, symbols, equations, standard abbreviations, significant figures and units — and it acknowledges its references in a few tidy words.
feynman.23: Connects Your question can sit in any content area you’ve studied. A fields investigation might measure how magnetic field strength falls off with distance from a wire, drawing on Action without contact; a motion investigation might measure how a projectile’s range depends on launch angle; a light investigation might measure how fringe spacing depends on slit separation, drawing on Light, matter, relativity. But in every single case the discipline of this Area of Study is the same — one continuous independent variable, controlled conditions, and honest uncertainty.

sagan.0: Here you become, for a time, a scientist in your own right. Unit 4 Area of Study 2 is a practical investigation of your own design, one that generates primary data on a question of your choosing drawn from fields, motion or light, governed by a single continuous independent variable. You conduct it, you record it in a logbook, you analyse it, and you report it as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. This page lays out the journey in order — what an investigation is, how to measure well, how raw data is transfigured into evidence, and how the whole enterprise is brought to a close and shared with others.
sagan.1: Connects Your investigation may set out across any of the three great territories of the course. A fields question draws on Action without contact and Electricity from fields; a light question draws on Light, matter, relativity; a motion question draws on the Unit 3 study of motion. The physics itself is not new to you. What is new — and what makes this the work of a scientist — is the discipline of generating evidence and defending it.
sagan.2: Every investigation begins, as all of science begins, with a question. The question must be one that measurement can answer, and it must isolate a single relationship: how one continuous quantity depends upon another. The quantity you deliberately change is the independent variable; the quantity you measure in response is the dependent variable. Every other quantity that might disturb the result is a controlled variable, and you must hold it constant throughout. In this Area of Study the independent variable is required to be continuous — free to take any value across a range, rather than confined to a handful of fixed categories.
sagan.3: A hypothesis is a testable prediction — your statement, made before a single datum is collected and grounded in the physics you know, of how the independent and dependent variables ought to be related. But understand this: the investigation does not set out to confirm your hypothesis. It sets out to test it, honestly and without flinching, and the data that returns may support it, partially support it, or fail to support it altogether.
sagan.4: The methodology is the kind of investigation you have chosen — here, a controlled practical experiment that generates primary data. The method is the procedure itself, in all its particulars: the equipment, the measurements you take, the range over which you vary the independent variable, the number of repeated readings, and the way you analyse what you gather. Primary data is data you yourself bring into being, and it may be quantitative, in the form of measured numerical values, or qualitative, in the form of recorded observations.
sagan.5: Key idea A valid experiment is one that investigates what it claims to investigate — nothing more, nothing less. Validity is threatened the moment a controlled variable is allowed to drift, for then the dependent variable answers to more than one cause and you can no longer say which is speaking; it is threatened, too, when the observer’s own bias slips quietly into the readings.
sagan.6: No measurement is ever exact — this is not a failing but a fact of the universe. The true value of a quantity is the value you would find if you could measure it perfectly, which you never can. Any real reading differs from that true value, and the gap between a measurement result and the true value is what we call the measurement error.
sagan.7: Accuracy and precision are not the same thing, and they are not synonyms — the difference between them is at the heart of measuring well. Accuracy is qualitative: a measurement is accurate when it lies close to the true value, and so you write “more accurate” or “less accurate”, but you never write an accuracy as a number. Precision is something else entirely — it is how closely your repeated measurements cluster about one another, the spread of the readings, and it tells you nothing at all about closeness to the true value. A set of readings can be exquisitely precise yet inaccurate, or accurate on average yet hopelessly imprecise.
sagan.8: Every instrument has a character of its own, described by its resolution and its uncertainty — and these, too, are distinct. Resolution is the smallest change in the measured quantity that the instrument can register as a perceptible change in its reading: a millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, a stopwatch reading to hundredths a resolution of 0.01 s. Uncertainty is a different claim altogether — it states the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as 20 ± 1 mm means the true value is expected to lie somewhere in the interval 19–21 mm. By VCE convention the uncertainty of a measuring instrument is half its smallest scale increment, so a millimetre ruler reads to ±0.5 mm and a 0.01 s stopwatch to ±0.005 s. Resolution and uncertainty are not one quantity wearing two names, and you must never report them as though they were.
sagan.9: Trap The uncertainty of an instrument is half its smallest scale increment, not the increment itself. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm but an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm.
sagan.10: Errors come in two kinds, distinguished by how they act upon the readings. A systematic error is more insidious — it pushes every reading away from the true value in the same direction by roughly the same amount, arising from calibration, from the instrument, or from the method itself, as in parallax or a zero error, and it afflicts accuracy. cox.16: Patterns are coaxed out of the data by graphing. The independent variable goes along the horizontal axis, the dependent variable up the vertical axis. Each plotted point carries its uncertainty bars: the vertical bar showing the uncertainty in the dependent variable, the horizontal bar the uncertainty in the independent variable, with the true value expected to lie somewhere within them. A line of best fit is drawn to follow the trend of all the points, threading within the uncertainty bars; it is not strung between the first and last points alone, not forced through any chosen point or through the origin, and not stretched beyond the range of your data. And a line that you expect to pass through the origin, but which doesn’t, is quietly telling you about a systematic error.
cox.17: Key idea Linearisation is a rather elegant trick: it transforms a curved relationship into a straight line, so that the gradient itself becomes a physical quantity. You transform the variable, and you transform its axis units along with it—for example, plotting t2 against distance with the axis labelled “time2 (s2)”. The gradient is then read from two points lying on the line and chosen far apart, its units are the vertical-axis units divided by the horizontal-axis units, and any ×10n axis scale factor is folded in.
cox.18: Once a pattern stands clear, you turn a critical eye on it. You identify the causes of uncertainty in the readings, state the assumptions you made and the limitations of both the data and the method, and ask honestly whether any of these could account for a departure of the data from the relationship you expected.
cox.19: A conclusion answers the question, and states whether the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” never appear: a single investigation establishes evidence, not proof—and there is a real honesty in that. The conclusion also sets out the limitations of the investigation and its implications, may point towards further work, but introduces no new information that wasn’t already laid out earlier.
cox.20: Before the conclusion is finally settled, the data and method are weighed for their limitations—sources of random and systematic error, restrictions on the range or number of readings, and assumptions that may not hold—and for each one, a realistic improvement is proposed.
cox.21: Key idea The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. The prescribed sections are: the title, which is the investigation question; your name; an introduction stating the aim, the hypothesis or prediction and the background physics; the methodology and methods; the results, shown so as to let the trend reveal itself; a discussion that interprets and evaluates the data, states limitations and improvements, relates the result to the physics and links back to the hypothesis; a conclusion that answers the question; and the references and acknowledgements.
cox.22: At the very heart of the poster sits a single Communication statement—one sentence that answers the investigation question. It takes up between 20 and 25 per cent of the poster area, so that a reader catches the central finding at a single glance. And the poster as a whole keeps to the conventions of science communication—correct terminology, symbols, equations, standard abbreviations, significant figures and units—and acknowledges its references succinctly.
cox.23: Connects The question can be set in any content area you’ve studied. A fields investigation might measure how magnetic field strength fades with distance from a wire, drawing on Action without contact; a motion investigation might measure how a projectile’s range depends on its launch angle; a light investigation might measure how fringe spacing depends on slit separation, drawing on Light, matter, relativity. But in every case the discipline of this Area of Study—one continuous independent variable, controlled conditions, honest uncertainty—is exactly the same.

greene.0: Here you become the physicist, not the reader of one. Unit 4 Area of Study 2 is a student-designed practical investigation that generates primary data on a question drawn from fields, motion or light, hinged on one continuous independent variable. You conduct it, record it in a logbook, analyse it, and report it as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. This page walks the journey in order: what an investigation is, how to measure well, how raw data crystallise into evidence, and how the whole thing is brought to a close and communicated.
greene.1: Connects The investigation may grow from any of the three content areas of the course. A fields question draws on Action without contact and Electricity from fields; a light question draws on Light, matter, relativity; a motion question draws on the Unit 3 study of motion. The physics itself is not new. What is new—and what you are really learning—is the discipline of generating and defending evidence.
greene.2: Every investigation begins with a single question, an aim. The question must be one that measurement can answer, and it must isolate a single relationship: how one continuous quantity rises or falls with another. The quantity you deliberately turn the dial on is the independent variable; the quantity that answers back, the one you measure, is the dependent variable. Everything else that might muddy the result is a controlled variable, held fixed throughout. In this Area of Study the independent variable must be continuous—free to take any value across a range, not locked into a few fixed categories.
greene.3: A hypothesis is a testable prediction of how the independent and dependent variables are related, written down before any data are collected and grounded in the relevant physics. Notice the subtlety: the investigation does not set out to confirm the hypothesis. It sets out to test it. And the data may support it, partially support it, or fail to support it.
greene.4: The methodology is the overall kind of investigation you choose—here, a controlled practical experiment that generates primary data. The method is the specific recipe: the equipment, the measurements taken, the range over which the independent variable is swept, the number of repeated readings, and the way the data are analysed. Primary data are the data you yourself generate, and they come in two flavours: quantitative, the measured numerical values, or qualitative, the recorded observations.
greene.5: Key idea A valid experiment investigates what it claims to investigate—nothing more, nothing less. Validity is threatened the moment a controlled variable is allowed to drift, so that the dependent variable is now responding to more than one cause, or when observer bias quietly tilts the readings.
greene.6: No measurement is ever exact. Imagine, for a moment, that you could measure a quantity perfectly: the value you would find is the true value. A real reading always falls a little to one side of it, and that gap—the difference between a measurement result and the true value—is the measurement error.
greene.7: Accuracy and precision sound like twins, but they are nothing of the kind. Accuracy is qualitative: a measurement is accurate when it sits close to the true value, and you write “more accurate” or “less accurate”, never an accuracy as a number. Precision is altogether different—it is how tightly repeated measurements cluster around each other, the spread of the readings, and it says nothing whatever about closeness to the true value. So a set of readings can be beautifully precise yet wrong, or accurate on average yet scattered.
greene.8: An instrument has two distinct fingerprints: its resolution and its uncertainty. Resolution is the smallest change in the measured quantity that nudges the reading at all—a millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, a stopwatch reading to hundredths a resolution of 0.01 s. Uncertainty is a different statement: it names the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as 20 ± 1 mm means the true value is expected to fall somewhere in the interval 19–21 mm. By VCE convention the uncertainty of a measuring instrument is half its smallest scale increment, so a millimetre ruler reads to ±0.5 mm and a 0.01 s stopwatch to ±0.005 s. Resolution and uncertainty are not the same quantity, and must never be reported as though they were.
greene.9: Trap The uncertainty of an instrument is half its smallest scale increment, not the increment itself. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm but an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm.
greene.10: Errors fall into two families, sorted by how they act on your readings. A random error is an unpredictable jitter that scatters the readings about the mean; it spoils precision, and you tame it by repeating the measurement and averaging. A systematic error is more insidious: it pushes every reading off the true value in the same direction by about the same amount, time after time, arising from calibration, from the instrument, or from the method—parallax, say, or a zero error—and it spoils accuracy. Averaging clears away random error only. It is powerless against systematic error, because every reading has been shoved the same way by the same fault, and the average is shoved with them.
greene.11: Trap Repeating a measurement and averaging reduces random error and improves precision. It does not reduce systematic error: a consistent offset survives the average unchanged. The fault must instead be found and removed.
greene.12: Repeating measurements lets you make two related judgements. Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements taken under identical conditions—the same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short stretch of time—and it gauges precision. Reproducibility asks a deeper question: how closely do the results agree when you change the conditions—a different observer, instrument, method or location? In doing so it puts a claimed precision to the test, can expose a hidden systematic error, and so loops the result back to accuracy.
greene.13: Before a single reading is taken, you bring the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines to bear: identifying the hazards, controlling the risks, and conducting the whole investigation responsibly.

This page walks through it in order: what an investigation actually is, how to measure without fooling yourself, how a pile of numbers becomes evidence, and how you finish the thing and tell people what you found.
munroe.1: Connects You can run the investigation on any of the three content areas of the course. A fields question pulls from Action without contact and Electricity from fields; a light question pulls from Light, matter, relativity; a motion question pulls from the Unit 3 study of motion. None of the physics is new. What’s new is the discipline of generating evidence and then defending it.
munroe.2: An investigation starts with an aim, or a question. The question has to be answerable by measurement, and it has to isolate exactly one relationship: how one continuous quantity depends on another. The quantity you deliberately change is the independent variable. The quantity you measure in response is the dependent variable. Every other quantity that could push the result around is a controlled variable, and you hold it constant the whole way through. In this Area of Study the independent variable has to be continuous, meaning it can take any value across a range, not just a handful of fixed categories.
munroe.3: A hypothesis is a testable prediction of the relationship between the independent and dependent variables. You write it down before you collect any data, and you base it on relevant physics. You are not trying to confirm it. You are trying to test it, and the data are allowed to support it, partially support it, or fail to support it. That last outcome is a real result too.
munroe.4: The methodology is the overall kind of investigation you chose — here, a controlled practical experiment that generates primary data. The method is the actual recipe: the equipment, the measurements you take, the range of the independent variable, how many times you repeat each reading, and how you analyse the numbers afterwards. Primary data are data you generate yourself. They can be quantitative, meaning measured numerical values, or qualitative, meaning recorded observations.
munroe.5: Key idea A valid experiment investigates what it says it investigates. Validity falls apart when a controlled variable is allowed to drift, so the dependent variable is now responding to more than one cause and you can’t tell which, or when observer bias leans on the readings.
munroe.6: No measurement is exact. The true value of a quantity is the value you’d get if you could measure the thing perfectly, which you can’t. A real reading differs from the true value, and that difference — between what you measured and what’s actually true — is the measurement error.
munroe.7: Accuracy and precision are two different things, and they are not synonyms. Accuracy is qualitative: a measurement is accurate if it’s close to the true value, so you write “more accurate” or “less accurate” and you never write an accuracy as a number. Precision is how close repeated measurements sit to each other — the spread of the readings — and it tells you nothing about how close they are to the true value. A set of readings can be precise and still wrong, or accurate on average and all over the place.
munroe.8: How an instrument behaves is described by its resolution and its uncertainty, and those are also two different things. Resolution is the smallest change in the measured quantity that actually nudges the reading: a millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, and a stopwatch reading to hundredths has a resolution of 0.01 s. The uncertainty states the interval the true value is expected to lie in. A result written 20 ± 1 mm means the true value is expected to sit somewhere in 19–21 mm. By VCE convention the uncertainty of a measuring instrument is half its smallest scale increment, so a millimetre ruler reads to ±0.5 mm and a 0.01 s stopwatch to ±0.005 s. Resolution and uncertainty are not the same quantity, and you must not report one as if it were the other.
munroe.9: Trap The uncertainty of an instrument is half its smallest scale increment, not the increment itself. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm but an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm.
munroe.10: Errors get classified by how they act on the readings. A random error is an unpredictable wobble that scatters the readings around the mean; it hits precision, and you knock it down by repeating the measurement and averaging. A systematic error pushes every reading off the true value in the same direction by about the same amount each time; it comes from calibration, from the instrument, or from the method — parallax, say, or a zero error — and it hits accuracy. Averaging only helps with random error. Averaging does nothing to a systematic error, because every reading was shoved the same way by the same fault, so the average gets shoved too.
munroe.11: Trap Repeating a measurement and averaging reduces random error and improves precision. It does not reduce systematic error: a consistent offset survives the average completely unchanged. You have to actually find the fault and remove it.
munroe.12: Repeating measurements lets you make two related judgements. Repeatability is how closely successive measurements agree under the same conditions — same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short time — and it tells you about precision. Reproducibility is how closely things agree when you deliberately change the conditions — a different observer, instrument, method or location — so it checks a claimed precision and can flush out a systematic error, which links the result back to accuracy.
munroe.13: Before you collect a single reading, you apply the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines: spot the hazards, control the risks, and run the investigation responsibly.
munroe.14: Evidence is data that bear on a hypothesis, a model or a theory, and that can support it or refute it. Primary data get their authority from how they were recorded. The logbook is a dated, written-as-you-go record of every reading, observation, instrument setting and change of plan, kept while the investigation is actually happening. It proves the primary data are yours, and it makes the work checkable by someone else.
munroe.15: When you report numerical values, the significant-figures conventions kick in. In a sum or difference, the result keeps the fewest decimal places of any value you used. In a product or quotient, the result keeps the fewest significant figures. You round only at the very last step, never on the intermediate values, so that rounding error doesn’t pile up on itself. Quantities go down with their correct symbols, units and standard abbreviations, written in the accepted notation, such as m s⁻¹.
munroe.16: Patterns show up when you graph the data. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis and the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Each plotted point carries uncertainty bars: the vertical bar shows the uncertainty in the dependent variable and the horizontal bar shows the uncertainty in the independent variable, with the true value expected to lie inside the bars. You draw a line of best fit to follow the trend of all the points, passing within the uncertainty bars. You do not just connect the first and last points, you do not force it through some point you like or through the origin, and you do not extend it past the range of your data. A line that’s expected to go through the origin but doesn’t is telling you there’s a systematic error.
munroe.17: Key idea Linearisation turns a curved relationship into a straight line, so that the gradient comes out equal to a physical quantity. You transform the variable and you transform its axis units along with it — for example, plotting t2 against distance with the axis labelled “time2 (s2)”. Then you read the gradient from two points that actually sit on the line and are chosen far apart; its units are the vertical-axis units divided by the horizontal-axis units, and you include any ×10n axis scale factor.
munroe.18: Once you’ve got a pattern, you evaluate it. You identify what caused the uncertainty in the readings, you state the assumptions you made and the limitations of both the data and the method, and you ask whether any of that could account for the data wandering off from the relationship you expected.
munroe.19: A conclusion answers the question and says whether the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. You do not use the words “proves” and “disproves”: a single investigation gives you evidence, not proof. The conclusion also states the limitations of the investigation and its implications, and it may point at further work, but it introduces no new information that wasn’t already on the table earlier.
munroe.20: Before you lock in the conclusion, you evaluate the data and method for limitations — sources of random and systematic error, limits on the range or number of readings, and assumptions that might not actually hold — and you propose a realistic improvement for each one.
munroe.21: Key idea The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. The sections are prescribed: the title, which is the investigation question; your name; an introduction stating the aim, the hypothesis or prediction and the background physics; the methodology and methods; the results, shown so the trend jumps out; a discussion that interprets and evaluates the data, states limitations and improvements, relates the result to the physics and links back to the hypothesis; a conclusion that answers the question; and the references and acknowledgements.
munroe.22: Right in the middle of the poster sits a single Communication statement — one sentence that answers the investigation question. It takes up between 20 and 25 per cent of the poster area, so a reader gets the central finding at a glance without hunting for it. The poster as a whole follows the conventions of science communication — correct terminology, symbols, equations, standard abbreviations, significant figures and units — and acknowledges its references without rambling.
munroe.23: Connects The question can sit in any content area you studied. A fields investigation might measure how magnetic field strength varies with distance from a wire, drawing on Action without contact; a motion investigation might measure how a projectile’s range depends on launch angle; a light investigation might measure how fringe spacing depends on slit separation, drawing on Light, matter, relativity. Whichever one you pick, the discipline of this Area of Study — one continuous independent variable, controlled conditions, honest uncertainty — doesn’t change.

bronowski.0: Here, at last, the physics turns and faces the one who does it. Unit 4 Area of Study 2 is a practical investigation of your own design, one that generates primary data on a question you draw from fields, motion or light, governed by a single continuous independent variable. You conduct it, you record it in a logbook, you analyse it, and you report it as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. This page sets out, in their proper order, the things you must understand: what an investigation is, how to measure well, how raw data is made to bear the weight of evidence, and how at last the work is brought to a conclusion and told to others.
bronowski.1: Connects The investigation may be raised on any of the three content areas of the course. A question about fields draws on Action without contact and Electricity from fields; a question about light draws on Light, matter, relativity; a question about motion draws on the Unit 3 study of motion. The physics itself is not new. What is new—and it is the harder thing—is the discipline of generating evidence and standing behind it.
bronowski.2: Every investigation begins with an aim, a question put to nature. The question must be one that measurement can answer, and it must isolate a single relationship: how one continuous quantity depends upon another. The quantity you deliberately change is the independent variable; the quantity that answers, that you measure in response, is the dependent variable. Every other quantity that might intrude upon the result is a controlled variable, and it is held constant throughout. In this Area of Study the independent variable must be continuous—free to take any value across a range, rather than confined to a few fixed categories.
bronowski.3: A hypothesis is a testable prediction of how the independent and dependent variables are related, set down before any data are collected and grounded in the physics you already know. You do not set out to confirm it; that is the temptation, and it must be resisted. You set out to test it—and the data, when it comes, may support the hypothesis, may support it in part, or may refuse to support it at all.
bronowski.4: The methodology is the kind of investigation you have chosen—here, a controlled practical experiment that generates primary data. The method is the particular path you walk: the equipment, the measurements you take, the range across which you vary the independent variable, the number of times you repeat each reading, and the way you analyse what you gather. Primary data is data you yourself generate, and it may be quantitative, the numerical values you measure, or qualitative, the observations you record.
bronowski.5: Key idea A valid experiment investigates what it claims to investigate—nothing more and nothing else. Validity is broken the moment a controlled variable is allowed to drift, so that the dependent variable now answers to more than one cause, or when the observer’s own expectation creeps into the readings.
bronowski.6: No measurement is ever exact; this is not a failing but a fact, and the honest experimenter begins from it. The true value of a quantity is what would be found if the quantity could be measured perfectly. Any real reading falls short of that, and the gap between a measurement result and the true value is the measurement error.
bronowski.7: Accuracy and precision are two different things, and to confuse them is to misunderstand measurement altogether. Accuracy is a judgement, not a number: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value, and you may call it “more accurate” or “less accurate”, but you never write an accuracy as a figure. Precision is how tightly repeated measurements cluster about one another—the spread of the readings—and it says nothing whatever about closeness to the true value. A set of readings can be precise and yet wrong, or right on average and yet scattered.
bronowski.8: An instrument is described by two things, its resolution and its uncertainty, and these too must be kept apart. Resolution is the smallest change in the measured quantity that the instrument can show: a millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, a stopwatch reading to hundredths a resolution of 0.01 s. The uncertainty names the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as 20 ± 1 mm declares that the true value is expected to lie somewhere in the interval 19–21 mm. By VCE convention the uncertainty of a measuring instrument is half its smallest scale increment, so a millimetre ruler reads to ±0.5 mm and a 0.01 s stopwatch to ±0.005 s. bronowski.9: Trap The uncertainty of an instrument is half its smallest scale increment—not the increment itself. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, but an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm.
bronowski.10: Errors are known by the way they act upon the readings. A random error is an unpredictable wavering that scatters readings about their mean; it touches precision, and it is tamed by repeating the measurement and taking the average. A systematic error is of another kind: it pushes every reading away from the true value in the same direction, by about the same amount each time, and it springs from calibration, from the instrument, or from the method itself—parallax, perhaps, or a zero error—and it touches accuracy. Averaging subdues random error and random error alone. It does nothing to a systematic error, because every reading has been shifted the same way by the same fault, and the fault survives the average untouched.
bronowski.11: Trap Repeating a measurement and averaging reduces random error and improves precision. It does not reduce systematic error: a consistent offset survives the average unchanged. The fault must instead be found and removed.
bronowski.12: Repeating measurements lets you make two related judgements. Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements taken under the same conditions—the same observer, the same instrument, method and place, over a short span of time—and it speaks to precision. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when the conditions are deliberately changed—another observer, another instrument, method or location—and so it puts a claimed precision to the test, can bring a hidden systematic error to light, and leads the result back toward the question of accuracy.

The logbook is the heart of this: a dated, contemporaneous record of every reading, every observation, every instrument setting and every change of plan, written down as the investigation unfolds. Rounding is left to the very last step, never performed on intermediate values, so that rounding error is not allowed to gather and compound. Every quantity is reported with its correct symbol, its unit and its standard abbreviation, written in the accepted notation, such as m s⁻¹.

Each plotted point carries its uncertainty bars: the vertical bar the uncertainty in the dependent variable, the horizontal bar the uncertainty in the independent variable, with the true value expected to lie within them. The conclusion also sets out the limitations of the investigation and its implications, and may point toward further work, but it brings in no new information that was not laid before the reader earlier.
