Accuracy is qualitative: a measurement is accurate if it’s close to the true value, and you write “more accurate” or “less accurate”, never an accuracy as a number. Precision is how close repeated measurements are to each other — the spread of the readings — and it tells you nothing about closeness to the true value. So a set of readings can be precise yet inaccurate, or accurate on average yet imprecise.
veritasium.8: An instrument is described by its resolution and its uncertainty — and these, too, are not the same thing. Resolution is the smallest change in the measured quantity that produces a perceptible change in the reading: a millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, and a stopwatch reading to hundredths a resolution of 0.01 s. The uncertainty states the interval in which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as 20 ± 1 mm means the true value is expected to lie in the interval 19–21 mm. By VCE convention the uncertainty of a measuring instrument is half its smallest scale increment, so a millimetre ruler reads to ±0.5 mm and a 0.01 s stopwatch to ±0.005 s. Resolution and uncertainty are different quantities, and reporting one as though it were the other is a mistake.
veritasium.9: Trap The uncertainty of an instrument is half its smallest scale increment, not the increment itself. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm but an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm.
veritasium.10: Errors get classified by how they act on your readings, and the distinction matters more than it first appears. A random error is an unpredictable variation that scatters readings about the mean; it affects precision, and you reduce it by repeating the measurement and averaging. A systematic error pushes readings away from the true value in a consistent direction by a similar amount every time; it comes from calibration, from the instrument, or from the method — parallax, a zero error — and it affects accuracy. Here’s the catch: averaging only fights random error. It does nothing to a systematic error, because every reading is shifted in the same direction by the same fault.
veritasium.11: Trap Repeating a measurement and averaging reduces random error and improves precision. It does not reduce systematic error: a consistent offset survives the average unchanged. The fault must instead be found and removed.
veritasium.12: Repeating measurements buys you two related judgements, and it’s worth keeping them apart. Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements under the same conditions — same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short time — and it evaluates precision. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when you change the conditions — a different observer, instrument, method or location — so it checks a claimed precision and can expose a systematic error, linking the result back to accuracy.
veritasium.13: Before a single data point is collected, you apply the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines: identifying hazards, controlling risks, and conducting the investigation responsibly.
veritasium.14: Data alone aren’t evidence. Evidence is data that bear on a hypothesis, a model or a theory, and that may support or refute it. Primary data earn their authority from the way they’re recorded. The logbook is a dated, contemporaneous record of every reading, observation, instrument setting and change of plan, written as the investigation proceeds. It authenticates the primary data as your own and makes the work able to be checked.
veritasium.15: When you report numerical values, the conventions of significant figures kick in. In a sum or difference the result keeps the fewest decimal places of any value used. In a product or quotient it keeps the fewest significant figures. And rounding happens only at the final step, never on intermediate values, so rounding error doesn’t quietly accumulate. Quantities are reported with their correct symbols, units and standard abbreviations, in the accepted notation, such as m s⁻¹.
veritasium.16: Patterns don’t leap out of a table — they leap out of a graph. The independent variable goes on the horizontal axis, the dependent variable on the vertical axis. Each plotted point carries uncertainty bars: the vertical bar shows the uncertainty in the dependent variable, the horizontal bar the uncertainty in the independent variable, with the true value expected to lie within the bars. A line of best fit follows the trend of all the points, passing within the uncertainty bars. It is not drawn through the first and last points only, is not forced through any chosen point or through the origin, and is not extended beyond the range of the data. And watch for this one: a line you expected to pass through the origin that doesn’t pass through it is telling you there’s a systematic error.
veritasium.17: Key idea Linearisation is a trick that turns a curved relationship into a straight line, so that the gradient becomes a physical quantity you can read off. You transform the variable — and you transform its axis units along with it — for example, plotting t2 against distance with the axis labelled “time2 (s2)”. The gradient is then read from two points lying on the line and chosen far apart, its units are the vertical-axis units divided by the horizontal-axis units, and any ×10n axis scale factor gets included.
veritasium.18: Once you’ve established a pattern, you turn on it critically. You identify the causes of uncertainty in the readings, state the assumptions you made and the limitations of the data and the method, and ask whether any of those could account for the data departing from the expected relationship.
veritasium.19: A conclusion answers the question and states whether the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. And notice the words you don’t get to use: not “proves”, not “disproves”. A single investigation establishes evidence, not proof. The conclusion also states the limitations of the investigation and its implications, and may point to further work — but it introduces no new information that wasn’t presented earlier.
veritasium.20: Before you settle the conclusion, you evaluate the data and method for limitations — sources of random and systematic error, restrictions on the range or number of readings, and assumptions that may not hold — and you propose a realistic improvement for each.
veritasium.21: Key idea The whole investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. The prescribed sections are: the title, which is the investigation question; your name; an introduction stating the aim, the hypothesis or prediction and the background physics; the methodology and methods; the results, shown so as to reveal the trend; a discussion that interprets and evaluates the data, states limitations and improvements, relates the result to the physics and links back to the hypothesis; a conclusion that answers the question; and the references and acknowledgements.
veritasium.22: At the centre of the poster sits one thing a reader should grasp instantly: a single Communication statement, one sentence, that answers the investigation question. It takes up between 20 and 25 per cent of the poster area, so the central finding lands at a glance. The poster as a whole follows the conventions of science communication — correct terminology, symbols, equations, standard abbreviations, significant figures and units — and acknowledges its references succinctly.
veritasium.23: Connects You can set the question in any content area you’ve studied. A fields investigation might measure how magnetic field strength varies with distance from a wire, drawing on Action without contact; a motion investigation might measure how a projectile’s range depends on launch angle; a light investigation might measure how fringe spacing depends on slit separation, drawing on Light, matter, relativity. The topic changes, but the discipline of this Area of Study doesn’t: one continuous independent variable, controlled conditions, honest uncertainty.

attenborough.0: Here, at last, the physics is yours to wield. Unit 4 Area of Study 2 is a practical investigation of your own design, one that generates primary data on a question you have chosen from fields, motion or light, turning on a single continuous independent variable. You conduct it, you record it in a logbook, you analyse it — and you tell its story in a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. This page sets out, in order, the journey ahead: what an investigation is, how to measure well, how data quietly become evidence, and how the whole endeavour is brought to a close and shared with the world.
attenborough.1: Connects Your investigation may dwell in any of the three great territories of the course. A fields question draws on Action without contact and Electricity from fields; a light question draws on Light, matter, relativity; a motion question draws on the Unit 3 study of motion. The physics, then, is not new to you. What is new — and what truly matters here — is the discipline of generating evidence, and of defending it.
attenborough.2: Every investigation begins, as all things do, with a question. And that question must be answerable by measurement; it must isolate a single relationship, and ask how one continuous quantity depends upon another. The quantity you deliberately change is the independent variable; the quantity that answers, that responds, is the dependent variable. Every other quantity that might disturb the result is a controlled variable, held perfectly constant throughout. In this Area of Study the independent variable must be continuous — free to take any value across a range, never confined to fixed categories.
attenborough.3: A hypothesis is a testable prediction of the relationship between the independent and dependent variables, declared before any data are gathered and grounded in the physics you know. But note this well: the investigation does not set out to confirm the hypothesis. It sets out to test it — and the data, in their honesty, may support it, partially support it, or refuse to support it at all.
attenborough.4: The methodology is the kind of investigation you have chosen — here, a controlled practical experiment that generates primary data. The method is the procedure itself: the equipment, the measurements taken, the range of the independent variable, the number of repeated readings, and the way the data are analysed. Primary data are data you generate with your own hands. They may be quantitative, in the form of measured numerical values, or qualitative, in the form of observations you have recorded.
attenborough.5: Key idea A valid experiment investigates what it claims to investigate, and nothing else. Validity is threatened the moment a controlled variable is allowed to wander, so that the dependent variable answers to more than one cause — or when the observer’s own bias steals into the readings.
attenborough.6: No measurement is ever exact. The true value of a quantity is the value you would find if the quantity could be measured perfectly — an ideal we approach but never quite reach. A real reading always differs from that true value, and the difference between a measurement result and the true value is the measurement error.
attenborough.7: Accuracy and precision are distinct, and they are not, despite appearances, the same thing. Accuracy is qualitative: a measurement is accurate if it sits close to the true value, and you write “more accurate” or “less accurate”, never an accuracy as a number. Precision is how closely repeated measurements gather about one another — the spread of the readings — and tells you nothing of their closeness to the truth. A set of readings can be precise yet inaccurate, or accurate on average yet imprecise.
attenborough.8: The character of an instrument is told by its resolution and its uncertainty, and these too are distinct. Resolution is the smallest change in the measured quantity that produces a perceptible change in the reading: a millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, a stopwatch reading to hundredths a resolution of 0.01 s. The uncertainty states the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as 20 ± 1 mm means the true value is expected to lie in the interval 19–21 mm. By VCE convention the uncertainty of a measuring instrument is half its smallest scale increment, so a millimetre ruler reads to ±0.5 mm and a 0.01 s stopwatch to ±0.005 s. Resolution and uncertainty are not the same quantity, and must never be reported as though they were.
attenborough.9: Trap Beware: the uncertainty of an instrument is half its smallest scale increment, not the increment itself. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm but an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm.
attenborough.10: Errors are known by how they act upon the readings. A random error is an unpredictable variation that scatters readings about the mean; it troubles precision, and is tamed by repeating the measurement and averaging. A systematic error, by contrast, pushes readings away from the true value in a single, consistent direction, by a similar amount each time; it springs from calibration, from the instrument, or from the method itself — parallax, perhaps, or a zero error — and it troubles accuracy. Averaging reduces random error alone. It does nothing to a systematic error, for every reading has been shifted in the same direction, by the same fault.
attenborough.11: Trap Repeating a measurement and averaging reduces random error and improves precision. But it does not reduce systematic error: a consistent offset survives the average untouched. The fault must instead be hunted down and removed.
attenborough.12: Repeating measurements supports two related judgements. Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements taken under the same conditions — the same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short span of time — and it speaks to precision. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when the conditions are changed — a different observer, instrument, method or location — and so it tests a claimed precision, can uncover a hidden systematic error, and binds the result back to accuracy.
attenborough.13: Before a single datum is collected, the experimenter attends to the relevant health, safety and ethical guidelines: identifying the hazards, controlling the risks, and conducting the whole investigation responsibly.
attenborough.14: Evidence is data that bear upon a hypothesis, a model or a theory, and that may support or refute it. Primary data draw their authority from the way they are recorded. The logbook is a dated, contemporaneous record — every reading, every observation, every instrument setting, every change of plan, written down as the investigation unfolds. It is what authenticates the primary data as your own, and what allows the work to be checked.
attenborough.15: When numerical values are reported, the conventions of significant figures hold sway. In a sum or difference the result keeps the fewest decimal places of any value used. In a product or quotient it keeps the fewest significant figures. Rounding is performed only at the final step, never upon intermediate values, so that rounding error cannot quietly accumulate. And quantities are reported with their correct symbols, units and standard abbreviations, written in the accepted notation, such as m s⁻¹.
attenborough.16: Patterns reveal themselves through graphing. The independent variable is plotted along the horizontal axis, the dependent variable up the vertical. Each plotted point carries its uncertainty bars: the vertical bar shows the uncertainty in the dependent variable, the horizontal bar the uncertainty in the independent variable, with the true value expected to lie somewhere within them. A line of best fit is drawn to follow the trend of all the points, passing within the uncertainty bars. It is not drawn through the first and last points alone, nor forced through any chosen point or through the origin, nor extended beyond the range of the data. And a line expected to pass through the origin that does not pass through it betrays a systematic error.
attenborough.17: Key idea Linearisation transforms a curved relationship into a straight line, so that the gradient itself becomes a physical quantity. You transform the variable, and you transform its axis units along with it — plotting, for example, t2 against distance with the axis labelled “time2 (s2)”. The gradient is then read from two points lying upon the line and chosen far apart; its units are the vertical-axis units divided by the horizontal-axis units; and any ×10n axis scale factor must be included.
attenborough.18: Once a pattern stands revealed, it is weighed. The experimenter names the causes of uncertainty in the readings, states the assumptions made and the limitations of the data and the method, and asks whether these might account for any departure of the data from the expected relationship.
attenborough.19: A conclusion answers the question, and states whether the evidence supports, partially supports or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” are never spoken here: a single investigation establishes evidence, not proof. The conclusion also states the limitations of the investigation and its implications, and may point toward further work — but it introduces no new information that was not laid out earlier.
attenborough.20: Before the conclusion is settled, the data and method are examined for their limitations — sources of random and systematic error, restrictions on the range or number of readings, assumptions that may not hold — and for each, a realistic improvement is proposed.
attenborough.21: Key idea The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. The prescribed sections are these: the title, which is the investigation question; your name; an introduction stating the aim, the hypothesis or prediction and the background physics; the methodology and methods; the results, shown so as to reveal the trend; a discussion that interprets and evaluates the data, states limitations and improvements, relates the result to the physics and links back to the hypothesis; a conclusion that answers the question; and the references and acknowledgements.
attenborough.22: At the very heart of the poster sits a single Communication statement — one sentence that answers the investigation question. It occupies between 20 and 25 per cent of the poster area, so that a reader takes in the central finding at a single glance. The poster as a whole observes the conventions of science communication — correct terminology, symbols, equations, standard abbreviations, significant figures and units — and acknowledges its references succinctly.
attenborough.23: Connects The question may be set in any content area you have studied. A fields investigation might measure how magnetic field strength varies with distance from a wire, drawing on Action without contact; a motion investigation might measure how a projectile’s range depends on launch angle; a light investigation might measure how fringe spacing depends on slit separation, drawing on Light, matter, relativity. Yet in every case the discipline of this Area of Study — one continuous independent variable, controlled conditions, honest uncertainty — remains forever the same.

greekepic.10: Errors are sorted by the manner in which they work upon the readings. cnpop.0: 第四单元学习领域2，是一场由你自己当导演的实践探究：围绕场、运动或者光里随便挑的一个问题，亲手刨出第一手数据，并且只能有一个连续的自变量。整个探究从头到尾都是你自己做、你自己记在日志本里、你自己分析，最后浓缩成一张不超过600字的科学海报交差。这一页就按顺序把话说明白：探究到底是个啥、怎么测才算测得漂亮、数据怎么熬成证据，以及这场探究最后怎么收尾、怎么讲给别人听。
cnpop.1: Connects 这场探究可以挑课程三大内容板块里的任意一个开刀。选场的问题，靠的是 Action without contact 和 Electricity from fields；选光的问题，靠 Light, matter, relativity；选运动的问题，就回头啃第三单元的运动那套。物理本身没新东西，真正新的，是“怎么造证据、怎么扛住质疑”这门硬功夫。
cnpop.2: 探究的开头，是一个目的或者一个问题。这问题必须能用测量来回答，而且只能锁死一个关系：一个连续的量到底怎么随另一个量变。你故意去拨动、去改变的那个量，叫自变量；跟着起反应、被你量下来的那个量，叫因变量。其他所有可能搅局、影响结果的量，统统算控制变量，全程死死按住不许动。在这个学习领域里，自变量必须是连续的——意思是它在一个范围内可以取任意值，而不是只能落在几个固定的档位上。
cnpop.3: 假设，就是一条可以被检验的预测，说的是自变量和因变量之间是个啥关系；它得在收数据之前、基于靠谱的物理先撂出来。探究的目的可不是去“确认”这条假设，而是去“检验”它——数据出来后，可能支持它、可能部分支持它，也可能根本不支持它。
cnpop.4: 方法论说的是你选的探究大类型——这里就是一场能产出第一手数据的受控实践实验。而方法说的是具体的操作流程：用啥器材、量哪些东西、自变量的取值范围、重复读数读几遍、数据又怎么分析。第一手数据就是你亲手造出来的数据，可以是定量的（一串测出来的数值），也可以是定性的（记下来的观察现象）。
cnpop.5: Key idea 一个有效（valid）的实验，研究的就是它声称要研究的那回事。一旦某个控制变量被放跑了，因变量就会同时被不止一个原因牵着走，有效性就崩了；或者观察者自己带了偏见、把读数带偏了，有效性也照样崩。
cnpop.6: 天下没有绝对精确的测量。一个量的真值，是假如这个量能被完美无瑕地测出来时会得到的那个值。而真实的读数永远会跟真值差那么一点点，测量结果和真值之间的这个差，就叫测量误差。
cnpop.7: 准确度（accuracy）和精密度（precision）是两码事，可别当成同义词混着用。准确度是定性的：一个测量值离真值近，就叫准确，你只能写“更准确”或者“不那么准确”，绝对不能把准确度写成一个数字。精密度说的是重复测量彼此之间挨得有多近——也就是读数的离散程度——它压根不告诉你离真值近不近。一组读数完全可能精密却不准确，也可能平均下来很准确、但一点都不精密。
cnpop.8: 一台仪器的脾气，由它的分辨率和它的不确定度来描述，这俩同样是两码事。分辨率是能让读数产生可察觉变化的、被测量的最小变动：毫米刻度尺的分辨率是 1 mm，能读到百分之一秒的秒表分辨率就是 0.01 s。不确定度则给出真值预计会落在的那个区间。一个写成 20 ± 1 mm 的结果，意思是真值预计落在 19–21 mm 这个区间里。按 VCE 的惯例，测量仪器的不确定度取它最小刻度增量的一半，所以毫米刻度尺读到 ±0.5 mm，0.01 s 的秒表读到 ±0.005 s。分辨率和不确定度根本不是同一个量，千万别当成一回事去报。
cnpop.9: Trap 仪器的不确定度是它最小刻度增量的一半，不是那个增量本身。一把毫米刻度尺的分辨率是 1 mm，可它的不确定度却是 ±0.5 mm。
cnpop.10: 误差按它们怎么折腾读数来分类。随机误差是一种没法预测的波动，让读数在平均值上下散开；它影响精密度，靠重复测量再取平均就能压下去。系统误差则让读数每次都朝同一个方向、差不多偏同样的量，跟真值拧着来；它来自校准、来自仪器，或者来自方法，比如视差、比如零点误差，它影响的是准确度。取平均只能干掉随机误差。取平均拿系统误差一点办法没有，因为每一个读数都被同一个毛病、朝同一个方向推走了同样多。
cnpop.11: Trap 重复测量再取平均，能压住随机误差、提升精密度。但它治不了系统误差：一个固定的偏移会原封不动地从平均里活下来。这毛病只能靠你把它揪出来、再除掉。
cnpop.12: 重复测量撑起两个相关的判断。重复性（repeatability）说的是在相同条件下连续测量彼此的吻合程度——同一个观察者、同一台仪器、同一种方法、同一个地点，在短时间内完成——它评估的是精密度。再现性（reproducibility）说的是把条件换掉之后的吻合程度——换个观察者、换台仪器、换种方法或者换个地点——所以它能检验你声称的精密度，还能揪出系统误差，把结果重新跟准确度挂上钩。
cnpop.13: 在动手收任何数据之前，你得先把相关的健康、安全和伦理准则套上：把危险源认出来、把风险控住，负责任地把这场探究做下去。
cnpop.14: 证据，就是跟某个假设、某个模型或者某个理论挂得上钩、能支持它或者推翻它的数据。第一手数据的权威感，来自它被记录的方式。日志本就是一份带日期、当场写下的实时记录，每一个读数、每一处观察、每一个仪器设置、每一次计划改动，都在探究进行的当口写下来。它替你证明这些第一手数据确实是你自己做出来的，也让整套工作经得起别人复查。
cnpop.15: 报数值的时候，有效数字那套规矩得照办。加减运算里，结果保留参与运算的各个值中小数位最少的那个。乘除运算里，结果保留有效数字最少的那个。四舍五入只在最后一步动手，中间值绝不提前舍——这样舍入误差才不会越滚越大。各个量要配上正确的符号、单位和标准缩写，按公认的写法写出来，比如 m s⁻¹。
cnpop.16: 规律是靠画图揪出来的。自变量画在横轴上，因变量画在纵轴上。每一个画上去的点都背着不确定度棒：竖着的那根表示因变量的不确定度，横着的那根表示自变量的不确定度，真值预计就落在这两根棒围出的范围里。最佳拟合线要顺着所有点的整体走势画，从各个不确定度棒里头穿过去；它可不是只连首尾两个点，不能被硬拽着穿过你挑中的某个点、也不能硬塞过原点，更不能伸到数据范围之外去。一条本该过原点却没过原点的线，就是在朝你喊：这儿有系统误差。
cnpop.17: Key idea 线性化，就是把一条弯弯的关系掰直成一条直线，好让斜率正好等于某个物理量。变量被变换，它那条轴的单位也得跟着一起变换——比方说，拿 t2 去对距离作图，轴就标成“time2 (s2)”。斜率这时候从线上挑两个隔得老远的点来读，它的单位是纵轴单位除以横轴单位，而且别忘了把轴上任何 ×10n 的比例因子也算进去。
cnpop.18: 规律一旦立住了，就轮到对它评头论足。你得把读数里造成不确定度的原因一一指出来，把自己做的假设、还有数据和方法的局限都摆明白，再掂量掂量：这些东西能不能解释数据为啥跟预期的关系对不上。
cnpop.19: 结论要回答问题，并且讲清楚证据到底是支持、部分支持、还是不支持你的假设。“证明（proves）”和“证伪（disproves）”这两个词不许用：单单一场探究立起来的是证据，不是铁证。结论还要说清这场探究的局限和它的意义，可以点一句往后还能怎么接着做，但绝不能往里塞前面没出现过的新信息。
cnpop.20: 在结论拍板之前，先把数据和方法拿出来评一评局限——随机误差和系统误差的来源、读数范围或者次数上的限制、还有那些可能站不住脚的假设——然后针对每一条，提出实打实、能落地的改进办法。
cnpop.21: Key idea 这场探究最后是按一张不超过600字的科学海报来打分的。规定的版块有这些：标题，也就是你的探究问题；学生姓名；引言，交代目的、假设或预测、还有背景物理；方法论和方法；结果，展示方式要能把趋势亮出来；讨论，负责解读和评价数据、说明局限和改进、把结果跟物理挂钩、再回扣到假设上；结论，回答问题；以及参考文献和致谢。
cnpop.22: 海报正中央坐镇的，是一句话的传达陈述（Communication statement），一句顶一句地回答探究问题。它得占掉海报面积的 20% 到 25%，好让读者一眼就把核心发现捞走。整张海报通篇都遵循科学传达的规矩——术语、符号、方程、标准缩写、有效数字和单位都得对——参考文献也得利落地致谢清楚。
cnpop.23: Connects 这个问题可以扎根在你学过的任意一个内容板块里。一个场的探究，可能去测磁场强度怎么随着离导线的距离变化，靠的是 Action without contact；一个运动的探究，可能去测抛体的射程怎么随发射角变化；一个光的探究，可能去测条纹间距怎么随缝间距变化，靠的是 Light, matter, relativity。但不管哪一种，这个学习领域的硬规矩——一个连续的自变量、受控的条件、诚实的不确定度——都一模一样。

confucius-en.0: The Master said. In Unit 4 Area of Study 2 you yourself design a practical investigation, and from your own hand you draw primary data upon a question chosen from fields, motion or light, governed by one continuous independent variable. You conduct the work, you set it down faithfully in a logbook, you analyse it, and at the last you render it as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. Let us proceed in order, as the craftsman lays his corner-posts: first, what an investigation is; then how to measure well; then how data become evidence; and finally how the work is concluded and made known to others.
confucius-en.1: Connects The investigation may rest upon any of the three content areas of the course. A question of fields draws upon Action without contact and Electricity from fields; a question of light upon Light, matter, relativity; a question of motion upon the Unit 3 study of motion. The physics, mark you, is not new. What is new is the discipline of generating evidence and standing ready to defend it.
confucius-en.2: An investigation begins with an aim, or a question. The question must be answerable by measurement, and must isolate a single relationship: how one continuous quantity depends upon another. The quantity you deliberately change is the independent variable; the quantity you measure in answer is the dependent variable. Every other quantity that might sway the result is a controlled variable, and is held constant throughout, as a steady hand holds the brush. In this Area of Study the independent variable must be continuous — able to take any value across a range, and not merely fixed categories.
confucius-en.3: A hypothesis is a testable prediction of the relationship between the independent and dependent variables, declared before any data are collected and founded upon relevant physics. Understand this well: the investigation does not set out to confirm the hypothesis. It sets out to test it, and the data may support, partially support, or fail to support it. The noble student does not love the prediction more than the truth.
confucius-en.4: The methodology is the kind of investigation you have chosen — here, a controlled practical experiment that yields primary data. The method is the particular procedure: the equipment, the measurements taken, the range of the independent variable, the number of repeated readings, and the manner in which the data are analysed. Primary data are data you have generated yourself, and may be quantitative, as measured numerical values, or qualitative, as recorded observations.
confucius-en.5: Key idea A valid experiment investigates what it claims to investigate, and no other thing. Validity is threatened when a controlled variable is suffered to change, so that the dependent variable answers to more than one cause; or when the bias of the observer creeps into the readings. Guard, then, against both.
confucius-en.6: No measurement is exact; let the experimenter be humble before this. The true value of a quantity is the value that would be found if the quantity could be measured perfectly. A real reading departs from the true value, and the difference between a measurement result and the true value is the measurement error.
confucius-en.7: Accuracy and precision are distinct, and the wise do not speak them as one. Accuracy is qualitative: a measurement is accurate if it lies close to the true value, and so you write “more accurate” or “less accurate”, but never an accuracy set down as a number. Precision is how close repeated measurements lie to one another — the spread of the readings — and tells you nothing of their nearness to the true value. A set of readings may be precise yet inaccurate, or accurate on the average yet imprecise.
confucius-en.8: The character of an instrument is told by its resolution and its uncertainty, which again are distinct. Resolution is the smallest change in the measured quantity that yields a perceptible change in the reading: a millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, and a stopwatch reading to hundredths a resolution of 0.01 s. The uncertainty states the interval within which the true value is expected to lie. A result written as 20 ± 1 mm means the true value is expected to lie in the interval 19–21 mm. By VCE convention the uncertainty of a measuring instrument is half its smallest scale increment, so a millimetre ruler reads to ±0.5 mm and a 0.01 s stopwatch to ±0.005 s. Resolution and uncertainty are not the same quantity, and must not be reported as though they were.
confucius-en.9: Trap The uncertainty of an instrument is half its smallest scale increment, not the increment itself. A millimetre ruler has a resolution of 1 mm, but an uncertainty of ±0.5 mm. Confuse not the two.
confucius-en.10: Errors are classed by the manner in which they act upon the readings. A random error is an unpredictable variation that scatters readings about the mean; it touches precision, and is lessened by repeating the measurement and averaging. A systematic error drives readings from the true value in one consistent direction, by a like amount each time; it springs from calibration, from the instrument, or from the method, as parallax or a zero error, and it touches accuracy. Averaging lessens random error alone. Averaging does not lessen systematic error, for every reading is shifted in the same direction by the same fault.
confucius-en.11: Trap Repeating a measurement and averaging lessens random error and betters precision. It does not lessen systematic error: a consistent offset survives the average unchanged. The fault must instead be sought out and removed.
confucius-en.12: Repeating measurements upholds two kindred judgements. Repeatability is the closeness of agreement between successive measurements taken under the same conditions — the same observer, instrument, method and location, over a short time — and it weighs precision. Reproducibility is the closeness of agreement when the conditions are changed — a different observer, instrument, method or location — and so it tests a claimed precision, may bring a systematic error to light, and binds the result back to accuracy.
confucius-en.13: Before a single datum is gathered, the experimenter heeds the relevant guidelines of health, safety and ethics: naming the hazards, mastering the risks, and conducting the investigation with a responsible hand.
confucius-en.14: Evidence is data that bear upon a hypothesis, a model or a theory, and that may support or refute it. Primary data take their authority from the manner of their recording. The logbook is a dated, contemporaneous record of every reading, observation, instrument setting and change of plan, written as the investigation proceeds. It attests the primary data as the experimenter’s own, and renders the work able to be checked by another.
confucius-en.15: When numerical values are reported, the conventions of significant figures hold sway. In a sum or difference the result keeps the fewest decimal places of any value used. In a product or quotient the result keeps the fewest significant figures. Rounding is performed only at the final step, never upon intermediate values, lest rounding error gather and accumulate. Quantities are reported with their correct symbols, units and standard abbreviations, written in the accepted notation, as m s⁻¹.
confucius-en.16: Patterns are made plain by graphing. The independent variable is plotted upon the horizontal axis, the dependent variable upon the vertical. Each plotted point bears its uncertainty bars: the vertical bar shows the uncertainty in the dependent variable, the horizontal bar the uncertainty in the independent variable, with the true value expected to lie within the bars. A line of best fit is drawn to follow the trend of all the points, passing within the uncertainty bars; it is not drawn through the first and last points alone, is not forced through any chosen point or through the origin, and is not extended beyond the range of the data. A line expected to pass through the origin that does not pass through it betrays a systematic error.
confucius-en.17: Key idea Linearisation transforms a curved relationship into a straight line, so that the gradient becomes a physical quantity. The variable is transformed, and its axis units are transformed along with it — for example, plotting t2 against distance with the axis labelled “time2 (s2)”. The gradient is then read from two points lying upon the line and chosen far apart; its units are the vertical-axis units divided by the horizontal-axis units, and any ×10n axis scale factor is included.
confucius-en.18: Once a pattern stands established, it is evaluated. The experimenter names the causes of uncertainty in the readings, states the assumptions made and the limitations of the data and the method, and asks whether these could account for any departure of the data from the expected relationship.
confucius-en.19: A conclusion answers the question, and states whether the evidence supports, partially supports, or does not support the hypothesis. The words “proves” and “disproves” are not used: a single investigation establishes evidence, not proof. The conclusion also states the limitations of the investigation and its implications, and may point toward further work; but it brings in no new matter that was not presented before.
confucius-en.20: Before the conclusion is settled, the data and method are weighed for their limitations — sources of random and systematic error, restrictions upon the range or number of readings, and assumptions that may not hold — and for each, realistic improvements are proposed.
confucius-en.21: Key idea The investigation is assessed as a scientific poster of no more than 600 words. The prescribed sections are these: the title, which is the investigation question; the student’s name; an introduction stating the aim, the hypothesis or prediction, and the background physics; the methodology and methods; the results, shown so as to reveal the trend; a discussion that interprets and evaluates the data, states limitations and improvements, relates the result to the physics, and links back to the hypothesis; a conclusion that answers the question; and the references and acknowledgements.
confucius-en.22: At the heart of the poster sits a single Communication statement of one sentence, which answers the investigation question. It occupies between 20 and 25 per cent of the poster area, so that a reader takes the central finding at a glance. The poster as a whole keeps the conventions of science communication — correct terminology, symbols, equations, standard abbreviations, significant figures and units — and acknowledges its references with brevity.
confucius-en.23: Connects The question may be set in any content area you have studied. A fields investigation might measure how magnetic field strength varies with distance from a wire, drawing upon Action without contact; a motion investigation might measure how a projectile’s range depends upon launch angle; a light investigation might measure how fringe spacing depends upon slit separation, drawing upon Light, matter, relativity. In every case the discipline of this Area of Study — one continuous independent variable, controlled conditions, honest uncertainty — remains one and the same.

confucius-zh.0: 子曰：第四单元第二学习领域者，学子自设之实践探究也。其取一问于场、运动或光，必择一连续之自变量，由是生其原始之数据。学子亲行之，录之于日志，析之以己手，终以科学海报陈之，文不过 600 字。此页有序而言之：先明探究为何物，次言善测之道，再示数据何以成证据，终述探究如何作结而以告人。如匠人筑室，必先正其序。
confucius-zh.1: Connects 探究可行于本课程三大内容之任一。问于场者，取诸 Action without contact 与 Electricity from fields；问于光者，取诸 Light, matter, relativity；问于运动者，取诸第三单元运动之学。其物理非新也。所新者，乃生证据、守证据之功夫耳。
confucius-zh.2: 子曰：探究之始，必有一旨或一问。其问当可以测量答之，且必专于一系：明一连续之量何以系于另一量。实验者所故意更易者，谓之自变量；因之而测得者，谓之因变量。凡他量之可扰其果者，皆控制变量也，须自始至终持之不变。此领域之自变量必为连续，谓其可取一域中之任值，非止于若干定类而已。
confucius-zh.3: 子曰：假说者，关乎自变量与因变量之关系，可验之预言也。其立于采数据之先，本于相关之物理。探究非欲证此假说为是，乃欲验之；而数据所示，或支持之，或部分支持之，或不能支持之。君子立言而后验之，不先信其所欲信也。
confucius-zh.4: 子曰：方法论者，所择探究之大类也——于此，乃生原始数据之受控实践实验。方法者，其具体之程序也：器材、所取之测量、自变量之范围、重复读数之多寡，以及析数据之道。原始数据者，实验者亲生者也，或为定量，即所测之数值；或为定性，即所录之观察。
confucius-zh.5: Key idea 有效之实验者，名其所究，即究其所名也。当一控制变量得以变易，使因变量应乎不止一因；或观者之偏私扰其读数：此皆有效性之所以受损也。
confucius-zh.6: 子曰：测量无尽善者。一量之真值，假使能测之尽美所当得者也。然实测之数，终异于真值；而测量之果与真值之差，谓之测量误差。
confucius-zh.7: 子曰：准确与精密，二者判然有别，非同义也。准确者，定性而言：测量近于真值，则谓之准；实验者但书“较准”“较不准”，而绝不以一数言其准确。精密者，谓重复测量彼此相近之度——即读数之散布也——而无以示其近真值与否。故一组读数，可精密而不准，亦可平均而准却不精密。
confucius-zh.8: 子曰：器之性，以其分辨力与不确定度述之，二者亦判然有别。分辨力者，所测之量能使读数生可觉之变之最小变化也：毫米尺之分辨力为 1 mm，秒表读至百分之一者，其分辨力为 0.01 s。不确定度者，言真值所当处之区间也。书一果为 20 ± 1 mm，谓真值当处 19–21 mm 之间。依 VCE 之惯例，测量器之不确定度为其最小刻度增量之半，故毫米尺读至 ±0.5 mm，而 0.01 s 之秒表读至 ±0.005 s。分辨力与不确定度，非一量也，不可混而报之，如混其名。
confucius-zh.9: Trap 器之不确定度，乃其最小刻度增量之半，非增量本身也。毫米尺之分辨力为 1 mm，而其不确定度则为 ±0.5 mm。名实之辨，不可不慎。
confucius-zh.10: 子曰：误差之分，视其何以作用于读数。随机误差者，不可预之变也，使读数散布于均值之周；其损精密，可以重复测量而取平均减之。系统误差者，使读数恒以一向、以相近之量异于真值；其起于校准、起于器、起于法，如视差、如零位之差，而损其准确。取平均者，唯减随机误差耳。取平均不能减系统误差，盖每一读数皆以同向、同量为同一弊所移也。
confucius-zh.11: Trap 重复测量而取平均，可减随机误差而进其精密。然不能减系统误差：恒定之偏移，过平均而不改。必寻其弊而除之，方可。
confucius-zh.12: 子曰：重复测量，所以助二相关之断也。重复性者，于同一情境下——同观者、同器、同法、同地、历时甚短——历次测量相合之度也，所以评精密。再现性者，于情境既变之时——异观者、异器、异法或异地——相合之度也，故可核所称之精密，且能揭系统误差，复系其果于准确。
confucius-zh.13: 子曰：采数据之先，实验者必行健康、安全与伦理之相关法度：辨其危，制其险，而行探究以负责之心。
confucius-zh.14: 子曰：证据者，关乎假说、模型或理论之数据也，可支持之，亦可驳之。原始数据之所以有威信，系乎其所录之道。日志者，记日之时之录也，凡读数、观察、器之设定、计之更易，皆于探究之进而书之。其证原始数据为实验者所自得，且使其功可受人核验。
confucius-zh.15: 子曰：报数值之时，有效数字之惯例当守。和差之果，存诸所用值中小数位最少者。积商之果，存其有效数字最少者。舍入唯于末步行之，绝不施于中间之值，使舍入之误不至累积。诸量必以其正确之符号、单位与标准之缩写报之，书以公认之记法，如 m s⁻¹。
confucius-zh.16: 子曰：规律者，作图以显之。自变量绘于横轴，因变量绘于纵轴。每一绘点皆负其不确定度棒：竖棒示因变量之不确定度，横棒示自变量之不确定度，真值当处棒内。最佳拟合线者，循众点之趋而作，穿乎不确定度棒之间；非但贯首末二点而已，非强使过某选定之点或过原点，亦不延出数据之范围。线本当过原点而不过者，乃系统误差之所示也。
confucius-zh.17: Key idea 线性化者，化曲为直，使其斜率即一物理之量也。其变量既变，则其轴之单位亦随之而变——譬如绘 t2 对距离，而标其轴曰“time2 (s2)”。斜率则取线上相距甚远之二点而读之，其单位为纵轴单位除以横轴单位，凡 ×10n 之轴尺度因子皆纳之。
confucius-zh.18: 子曰：规律既立，则评之。实验者辨读数中不确定度之诸因，陈其所作之设想，与数据及方法之局限，且思此数者，能否解数据偏离所期关系之所由也。
confucius-zh.19: 子曰：结论者，答其问也，且明证据之或支持、或部分支持、或不支持其假说。“证实”“证伪”之辞不可用：一探究但立证据，非立证明也。结论亦陈探究之局限与其意涵，或可示其后续之功，然不引前所未陈之新事。君子言有所据，不溢其辞。
confucius-zh.20: 子曰：结论既定之前，数据与方法当评其局限——随机与系统误差之诸源、范围或读数之多寡之所限、可不立之设想——而于每一者，皆议其切实可行之改进。
confucius-zh.21: Key idea 探究以科学海报评之，文不过 600 字。其当备之节有：题，即探究之问；学子之名；引言，陈其旨、其假说或预言、其背景物理；方法论与方法；结果，陈之以显其趋；讨论，释而评其数据、陈其局限与改进、系其果于物理而复连于假说；结论，答其问；终则参考与致谢。有序而陈，则览者明矣。
confucius-zh.22: 子曰：海报之中央，置一句之传达陈述，以答探究之问。其占海报之面积二十至二十五之百分，使览者一望而得其中心之发现。海报之全，循科学传达之惯例——正确之术语、符号、方程、标准缩写、有效数字与单位——且简而明地致谢其所参之文。
confucius-zh.23: Connects 其问可设于所学之任一内容领域。问于场者，或测磁场强度何以随距导线之远而变，取诸 Action without contact；问于运动者，或测一抛体之射程何以系于发射之角；问于光者，或测条纹间距何以系于狭缝之分隔，取诸 Light, matter, relativity。然无论何者，此领域之功夫——一连续之自变量、受控之条件、诚实之不确定度——其一也。
